generatedFrom: ITI VPAT 2.5 Revised Section 508 Edition (Nov 2023). Criterion text reproduced from 36 CFR Part 1194 and WCAG 2.0. Verify against ITI source before publishing a real ACR.

chapters.title: Chapter 3: Functional Performance Criteria (FPC)
chapters.description: When ICT is required to conform to a Functional Performance Criterion, it shall meet the requirements for the function as described in §302.

criteria.text: Where a visual mode of operation is provided, ICT shall provide at least one mode of operation that does not require user vision.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can a user who is fully blind operate your product end-to-end using a screen reader (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, etc.)? Are there any flows — onboarding, search, settings, billing — where vision is required?

criteria.text: Where a visual mode of operation is provided, ICT shall provide at least one mode of operation that magnifies, one mode that reduces the field of vision required, and one mode that allows user control of contrast.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can a user with low vision use your product — does it support OS-level zoom up to 200% without loss of content, allow text resizing, and respect high-contrast modes?

criteria.text: Where a visual mode of operation is provided, ICT shall provide at least one mode of operation that does not require user perception of color.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Is colour ever the only way information is conveyed in your product? E.g. a red badge for errors with no icon or label, or a chart that only uses colour to distinguish series.

criteria.text: Where an audible mode of operation is provided, ICT shall provide at least one mode of operation that does not require user hearing.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your product use audio at all (alerts, video, voice prompts)? If so, is the same information available without sound — visual indicators, captions, transcripts?

criteria.text: Where an audible mode of operation is provided, ICT shall provide at least one mode of operation that enhances audio, allows user control of volume independent of the platform, and reduces background sound.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your product plays audio, can the user adjust its volume independently of system volume? Are there background sounds that compete with primary audio?

criteria.text: Where speech is used for input, control, or operation, ICT shall provide at least one mode of operation that does not require user speech.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your product require speech input anywhere — voice search, voice commands, dictation? If so, is there a non-speech equivalent?

criteria.text: ICT shall provide at least one mode of operation that does not require fine motor control or simultaneous manual operations.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are there any product interactions that require precise mouse control, drag-and-drop without a keyboard alternative, multi-finger gestures, or holding multiple keys at once?

criteria.text: ICT shall provide at least one mode of operation that is operable with limited reach and strength.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does the product require sustained input (holding a button), high force (heavy keystrokes), or movements outside a small area of the screen?

criteria.ref: 302.9 With Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities
criteria.text: ICT shall provide features making its use by individuals with limited cognitive, language, and learning abilities simpler and easier.
criteria.interviewQuestion: What does your product do to support users with cognitive disabilities? Plain language, clear error messages, progressive disclosure, no time pressure, predictable navigation?

chapters.title: Chapter 4: Hardware
chapters.description: Hardware criteria from 36 CFR Part 1194. For pure web/SaaS products with no hardware component, mark every row Not Applicable and note "Web/SaaS product — no hardware component" in the remarks.

criteria.text: Characters displayed on the screen shall have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against the background.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your product include any hardware with an integrated display? If so, does the displayed text meet at least 3:1 contrast against the background?

criteria.text: Characters on screen shall be in a sans serif font.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does the hardware display use a sans-serif font for its characters?

criteria.text: Characters shall not be displayed in uppercase only.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does any text on the hardware display appear in all-uppercase? If so, is mixed-case available as an alternative?

criteria.text: Characters displayed on the screen shall comply with the display requirements of Section 402.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does the hardware screen have a non-glare coating or equivalent anti-reflection properties?

criteria.text: Where provided, braille shall conform to ANSI/BHS S2-2010.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware include a braille display or braille output? If so, does it conform to ANSI/BHS S2-2010?

criteria.text: Braille cells shall have a separation of 2.5 mm center to center between cells.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware has a braille output component, are the braille cells spaced at 2.5 mm center-to-center?

criteria.text: Characters on variable message signs shall comply with section 703.7 Visual Characters of ICC A117.1-2009.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your product include any variable message signs (e.g., on kiosks or displays)? If so, do characters meet ICC A117.1-2009 visual character requirements?

criteria.text: Where provided, icons shall be accompanied by text or be programmatically determinable.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do any hardware controls use icons only? If so, is each icon accompanied by a visible text label or programmatically determinable description?

criteria.text: ICT with a closed functionality that relies on speech output shall ensure the speech output meets the requirements of 402.6.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware provide speech output (e.g., a kiosk reading menus aloud)? Is speech output available in all modes of operation?

criteria.text: ICT that provides speech output shall provide volume control and output amplification.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware provides speech output, can users adjust the volume? Is there a headphone jack for private listening?

criteria.text: Speech output shall be delivered through a mechanism that is readily available to all users, including those who need it for accessibility.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Is the speech output on your hardware always available and not dependent on a special mode that users must activate?

criteria.text: Non-speech audio outputs shall not be the only means of conveying information.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware use sounds (beeps, tones) to convey information? Is that information also available visually or through touch?

criteria.text: ICT that transmits or converts information or communications shall not remove non-proprietary information needed for accessibility or shall restore it upon delivery.
criteria.interviewQuestion: When your hardware transmits or converts data, does it preserve accessibility metadata (e.g., alt text in documents, captions in video streams)?

criteria.text: The same degree of privacy of input and output shall be provided to individuals with disabilities as is provided to others.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware has accessibility modes (e.g., speech output for a blind user), can that user use them with the same privacy as a sighted user typing silently?

criteria.text: Where data connections used for input and output are provided, at least one of each type of connection shall conform to industry-standard non-proprietary formats.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware expose any data connections? If so, is at least one of each type (USB, audio jack, etc.) using an industry-standard, non-proprietary format?

criteria.text: ICT shall be operable with at least one hand.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can all hardware controls be operated with one hand, without requiring simultaneous two-hand operations?

criteria.text: Mechanically operated controls and keys shall be discernible by touch from each other and from surrounding surfaces.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are the physical buttons and keys on your hardware distinguishable by touch — do they have distinct shapes, textures, or spacing so a blind user can locate them?

criteria.text: Touch-operated controls and keys shall be discernible by touch from surrounding surfaces without activating the control or key.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware uses a touchscreen, can users explore the screen by touch to find controls without accidentally activating them?

criteria.text: Where provided, input controls shall comply with 407.3.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are all input controls on your hardware (buttons, dials, sliders) operable without vision and distinguishable by touch?

criteria.text: Where ICT provides key repeat functionality, the delay before the repeated keystroke shall be adjustable to at least 2 seconds. The key repeat rate shall be adjustable to 2 seconds per character.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware keyboard supports key repeat, can users set the repeat delay to at least 2 seconds to prevent accidental key repetitions?

criteria.text: Where provided, individual alphabetic keys shall be arranged in a QWERTY keyboard layout and the F and J keys shall be tactilely distinct from the other keys.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware has a physical keyboard, are the keys in QWERTY layout with tactile indicators on F and J?

criteria.text: Where a function uses a linear, rather than arbitrary, keying sequence, the sequence shall be non-ambiguous.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Where your hardware requires a specific key sequence (like a PIN entry), is the sequence unambiguous and does not require simultaneous key presses?

criteria.text: Where provided, tickets, fare cards, and passes shall have an orientation that is tactilely discernible if orientation is important to further use of the ticket.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware dispenses or accepts physical tickets or cards, can a user with vision impairment tell which orientation to insert or use them?

criteria.text: Where a forward reach is provided, the high forward reach shall be no higher than 48 inches (1220 mm) and no lower than 15 inches (380 mm) above the floor.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are all operable controls on your hardware reachable from a seated position — between 15 and 48 inches from the floor?

criteria.text: Where a clear floor space allows a parallel approach and the side reach is unobstructed, the high side reach shall be no higher than 48 inches and the low side reach no lower than 15 inches above the floor.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Where side reach is available, are all controls reachable within the 15–48 inch height range for a user in a wheelchair approaching from the side?

criteria.text: Where the side reach is over an obstruction no deeper than 10 inches and no higher than 34 inches, the high side reach shall be no higher than 46 inches.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Where an obstruction blocks side reach, are all controls within the adjusted reach range (no higher than 46 inches)?

criteria.text: Where a clear floor space allows a forward approach and the forward reach is unobstructed, the high forward reach shall be no higher than 48 inches and no lower than 15 inches.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Where forward reach is unobstructed, are all controls within the 15–48 inch height range from the floor?

criteria.text: Where the forward reach is over an obstruction between 20 and 25 inches above the floor, the high forward reach shall be no higher than 44 inches.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Where an obstruction blocks forward reach at 20–25 inches, are all controls no higher than 44 inches from the floor?

criteria.text: Where provided, status indicators shall be discernible visually and by touch or sound.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do your hardware status indicators (power, caps lock, charging) provide both a visual and a non-visual indicator (tactile bump, audible tone, or speech)?

criteria.text: Where a display screen is provided, it shall be visible from a point 40 inches (1015 mm) above the floor to the centerline of the screen.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Is the display screen on your hardware visible to a user in a wheelchair seated approximately 40 inches from the floor?

criteria.text: Where provided, status indicators shall be discernible visually and by touch or sound.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do any non-audible status indicators on your hardware (LEDs, icons) also have an audible or tactile equivalent?

criteria.text: ICT shall not use color as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element.
criteria.interviewQuestion: On your hardware's displays or status indicators, is color ever the only means of conveying information (e.g., a red LED only, with no text or shape difference)?

criteria.text: ICT that provides two-way voice communication shall provide a gain adjustable up to a minimum of 18 dB above the manufacturer's default volume.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware supports voice calls, can users amplify the receive volume at least 18 dB above the default?

criteria.text: ICT that provides two-way voice communication shall include a volume control that allows the user to incrementally adjust volume by at least 12 dB above the default.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware's volume control allow at least 12 dB of gain adjustment above the manufacturer's default?

criteria.text: If ICT provides a mechanism to adjust the volume, it shall include a function that automatically resets the volume to the default level after every use for non-telephone ICT.
criteria.interviewQuestion: After a call ends or a session finishes, does your hardware automatically reset the volume to the default level to protect the next user's hearing?

criteria.text: ICT that provides two-way voice communication shall not use digital audio compression in a way that prevents the use of hearing aid couplers and loop systems.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware's audio codec allow hearing aid telecoil coupling and inductive loop connectivity?

criteria.text: Where ICT provides real-time two-way voice communication, ICT shall include functions allowing the user to manage calling and status information with a single hand.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can a user answer, end, mute, and manage calls on your hardware using only one hand?

criteria.text: Where ICT provides video communications capabilities, ICT shall support the ability to transmit and receive lip-readable video.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware supports video calls, does it transmit and receive video at quality sufficient for lip-reading (at least QCIF 30fps)?

criteria.text: ICT shall support all commonly used TTY signal protocols.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware support TTY/TDD connections for users who communicate via text telephone?

criteria.text: The baud rate for TTY shall conform to the minimum of 300 baud.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware supports TTY, does it support at least 300 baud transmission?

criteria.text: ICT shall support the use of all TTY protocols required by the FCC.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware support all FCC-required TTY protocols?

criteria.text: ICT shall support voice terminal protocols used in conjunction with TTY devices.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware support voice carryover (VCO) and hearing carryover (HCO) TTY modes?

criteria.text: ICT that displays or processes video with synchronized audio shall support the processing and rendering of closed captions.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware plays video with synchronized audio, does it decode and display CEA-608/708 closed captions?

criteria.text: ICT that displays or processes video with synchronized audio shall support the playback of audio descriptions.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware support playback of secondary audio program (SAP) or description audio tracks?

criteria.text: ICT that provides closed captions shall support all caption formats as required by 414.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your hardware includes a display, does it properly render all supported closed caption styles and positions?

criteria.text: ICT that provides real-time two-way voice communication shall support audio descriptions in real-time.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware's voice communication system support real-time audio description for video calls?

criteria.text: ICT shall provide the user with the ability to turn closed captions on and off.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware provide accessible controls for the user to enable or disable closed captions?

criteria.text: ICT shall provide the user with the ability to turn audio descriptions on and off.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your hardware provide accessible controls for the user to enable or disable audio descriptions?

chapters.title: Chapter 5: Software
chapters.description: Software criteria from 36 CFR Part 1194, Chapters 5 (501–504). These apply to software with a user interface. For web-only products, 501.1 applies via WCAG; 502.x applies to any platform software that exposes an AT interface.

criteria.text: Where software is a platform application with a user interface or has a user interface, it shall conform to the WCAG 2.0 Level A and AA Success Criteria and Conformance Requirements in Chapter 5.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Confirm whether your product has a user interface (the answer is almost always yes for web/SaaS). This row anchors WCAG conformance in the software chapter.

criteria.text: Platform software shall not override accessibility features that are defined in the platform documentation as accessibility features, except for those features documented as requiring user action.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your product ever disable OS accessibility features — turn off the screen reader, ignore high-contrast mode, suppress reduced-motion preferences?

criteria.text: Software shall not disrupt platform features that are defined in the platform documentation as accessibility features.
criteria.interviewQuestion: When your product runs, does it in any way interfere with OS or platform accessibility features — screen readers, zoom, sticky keys, etc.?

criteria.text: The object role, state(s), properties, boundary, name, and description shall be programmatically determinable.
criteria.interviewQuestion: For every UI control in your product, can assistive technology (screen reader, voice control) determine its role (button, link, etc.), current state (checked, expanded), and accessible name?

criteria.text: States and properties that can be set by the user shall be capable of being set by assistive technology.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can assistive technology programmatically change the state of controls (check a checkbox, toggle a switch, select an option) rather than only reading them?

criteria.text: If an object is in a data table, the occupied rows and columns, and any headers associated with those rows or columns, shall be programmatically determinable.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are data tables in your product built with proper header associations so a screen reader can announce 'Column: Revenue, Row: Q1' when reading a cell?

criteria.text: Any current value(s), and any set or range of allowable values associated with an object, shall be programmatically determinable.
criteria.interviewQuestion: For sliders, spinners, and range inputs, does the AT know the current value, minimum, and maximum? E.g. aria-valuenow, aria-valuemin, aria-valuemax.

criteria.text: Values that can be set by the user shall be capable of being set by assistive technology.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can AT programmatically adjust the value of sliders, number inputs, and other range controls — not just read them?

criteria.text: Any relationship that a component has as a label for another component, or of being labeled by another component, shall be programmatically determinable.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are label-to-control associations explicit in the accessibility tree (htmlFor/id pairing, aria-labelledby)? Can AT always tell which label belongs to which control?

criteria.text: Any hierarchical (parent-child) relationship that a component has shall be programmatically determinable.
criteria.interviewQuestion: For tree views, nested lists, accordions, and menus, are parent-child relationships exposed in the accessibility tree (aria-owns, aria-level, nested role structures)?

criteria.text: The content of text objects, text attributes, and the boundary of text rendered to the screen, shall be programmatically determinable.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can AT access all rendered text in the UI — including text in custom canvas-based renderers, SVG labels, and CSS-generated content?

criteria.text: Text that can be set by the user shall be capable of being set by assistive technology.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can voice control and switch access software insert text into all editable fields in your product?

criteria.text: A list of actions that can be executed on an object shall be programmatically determinable.
criteria.interviewQuestion: For context menus and action menus, are the available actions exposed to AT so a user can invoke them without using the mouse?

criteria.text: The position of the keyboard navigation cursor shall be programmatically determinable.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can AT always determine where keyboard focus is — even inside custom widgets like rich text editors, date pickers, or data grids?

criteria.text: If keyboard navigation focus can be set by the user, the focus cursor shall be capable of being set by assistive technology.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can AT programmatically move keyboard focus to specific elements (e.g., to shift focus to a newly opened dialog)?

criteria.text: Notification of events relevant to user interactions, including but not limited to, changes in the component's state(s), value, name, description, or boundary, shall be available to assistive technology.
criteria.interviewQuestion: When UI state changes dynamically — a panel expands, a form field becomes required, a status changes — is that change announced to AT via live regions or state change events?

criteria.text: Platform software shall conform to the requirements in ANSI/HFES 200.2 (Human Factors Engineering of Software User Interfaces — Part 2: Accessibility).
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your product is a platform (hosting other software or services), does it provide accessibility APIs and services that hosted applications can leverage?

criteria.text: Applications shall permit user preferences from platform settings for color, contrast, font type, font size, and focus cursor.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your product respect the user's OS-level preferences for fonts, colours, contrast, and focus indicator? Or does it force its own values regardless of system settings?

criteria.text: Where an application provides an alternative user interface that acts as assistive technology, the application shall use platform and other industry-standard accessibility services.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your product includes a built-in accessibility mode or alternative interface, does it expose that interface through standard accessibility APIs?

criteria.ref: 503.4 User Controls for Captions and Audio Description
criteria.text: Where ICT displays video with synchronized audio, ICT shall provide user controls for closed captions and audio descriptions at the same level of prominence as other controls.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your product play video? If so, are the caption and audio description toggle controls easily accessible — not buried in a settings menu?

criteria.text: Where user controls are provided for volume adjustment, ICT shall provide user controls for the selection of captions at the same level of prominence as those for volume or program selection.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are caption controls as prominent as volume controls in your media player — e.g., a CC button in the same control bar, not hidden in a settings panel?

criteria.text: Where user controls are provided for program selection, ICT shall provide user controls for the selection of audio descriptions at the same level of prominence as those for volume or program selection.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are audio description controls as accessible as program selection controls in your media player?

criteria.text: Where authoring tools provide a mode of operation to create or edit content, the authoring tool shall enable the creation of content that conforms to Level A and Level AA Success Criteria of WCAG 2.0.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your product allow users to create content others will consume (reports, dashboards, exports)? If so, does the tool produce accessible output by default?

criteria.ref: 504.2.1 Preservation of Accessibility Information in Format Conversion
criteria.text: Where authoring tools provide the ability to convert content from one format to another, accessibility information shall be preserved in the conversion to the extent that the target format supports the accessibility information.
criteria.interviewQuestion: When your product exports content (PDF, DOCX, CSV, HTML), does it preserve accessibility metadata — alt text, headings, table structure — in the output format?

criteria.text: Where an authoring tool provides the ability to export in PDF format, the authoring tool shall also support the production of tagged PDF.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your product exports PDF files, does it produce tagged PDF (with document structure, headings, alt text) rather than untagged image-based PDFs?

criteria.text: Where the authoring tool provides prompts, the prompts shall provide information and guidance on how to create accessible content.
criteria.interviewQuestion: When users create content in your product, does it prompt them to add accessibility information (e.g., asking for alt text when an image is inserted)?

criteria.text: Where a template is provided, the template shall comply with the applicable provisions in this document.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your product provides content templates (document templates, slide layouts, email templates), are those templates themselves accessible and do they encourage accessible outputs?

chapters.title: Chapter 6: Support Documentation and Services
chapters.description: Documentation and support service obligations under 36 CFR Part 1194.

criteria.text: Documentation shall list and explain how to use the accessibility and compatibility features required by Chapters 4 and 5.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your user documentation describe the product's accessibility features and how to use them (keyboard shortcuts, screen-reader behaviour, contrast modes)?

criteria.text: Documentation provided in electronic format, including Web-based self-service support, shall conform to Level A and Level AA Success Criteria of WCAG 2.0.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Is your product documentation itself accessible? PDFs tagged properly, web help pages meeting WCAG 2.0 AA?

criteria.ref: 602.4 Alternate Formats for Non-Electronic Support Documentation
criteria.text: Where support documentation is only provided in non-electronic formats, alternate formats usable by individuals with disabilities shall be provided upon request.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If any of your support documentation is only available in print, can a user request it in an accessible format (large print, braille, electronic)?

criteria.ref: 603.2 Information on Accessibility and Compatibility Features
criteria.text: ICT support services shall include information on the accessibility and compatibility features that are required by Chapters 4 and 5.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can a customer contact your support team with an accessibility question and get a competent answer? Does support documentation include accessibility topics?

criteria.text: Support services shall accommodate the communication needs of individuals with disabilities.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can a user with a disability reach support through an accessible channel (TTY, video relay, accessible chat, email if phone is inaccessible)?

chapters.title: WCAG 2.0 Level A Success Criteria
chapters.description: All Level A criteria are required by Section 508 for software and web content.

criteria.text: All non-text content that is presented to the user has a text alternative that serves the equivalent purpose, except for the situations listed in WCAG.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do all images, icons, charts, and non-text UI controls have appropriate text alternatives? Are decorative images marked so screen readers skip them?

criteria.text: For prerecorded audio-only and video-only media, an alternative for time-based media is provided that presents equivalent information.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do you have any audio-only or video-only (silent) prerecorded media? If so, is there a text transcript (audio) or text/audio description (video)?

criteria.text: Captions are provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do all prerecorded videos with sound (training, marketing, demos shown in-product) have captions?

criteria.ref: 1.2.3 Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded) (Level A)
criteria.text: An alternative for time-based media or audio description of the prerecorded video content is provided for synchronized media.
criteria.interviewQuestion: For videos where important content is visual-only (no narration), is there either an audio description track or a text alternative describing what's on screen?

criteria.text: Information, structure, and relationships conveyed through presentation can be programmatically determined or are available in text.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are headings marked up as headings, lists as lists, tables with proper headers, form fields with proper labels? Visual structure should be reflected in the DOM/accessibility tree.

criteria.text: When the sequence in which content is presented affects its meaning, a correct reading sequence can be programmatically determined.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If you read the DOM order (or tab through the page), does the content make sense in that order — or does CSS rearrange things into a different visual sequence that breaks reading order?

criteria.text: Instructions for understanding and operating content do not rely solely on sensory characteristics such as shape, size, visual location, orientation, or sound.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do any of your instructions say things like 'click the button on the right' or 'press the green button' without also identifying the element by name?

criteria.text: Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Is colour ever the only differentiator — required fields in red only, status pills with no text, charts series identified only by colour?

criteria.text: If any audio on a Web page plays automatically for more than 3 seconds, a mechanism is available to pause or stop the audio, or to control audio volume independently from the overall system volume.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does any page or screen play audio automatically? If so, is there an obvious way to stop or mute it?

criteria.text: All functionality of the content is operable through a keyboard interface without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Can every action in your product be performed with only the keyboard — no mouse required? Drag-and-drop, modal dialogs, tooltips, custom widgets?

criteria.text: If keyboard focus can be moved to a component of the page using a keyboard interface, then focus can be moved away from that component using only a keyboard interface.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are there any modals, embedded widgets, or custom controls where keyboard focus gets stuck and can't escape with Tab/Escape?

criteria.text: For each time limit that is set by the content, the user can turn off, adjust, or extend the time limit.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are there any time limits — session timeouts, auto-dismissing notifications, countdowns? Can users extend or disable them?

criteria.text: For moving, blinking, scrolling, or auto-updating information, users can pause, stop, or hide it.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do you have any auto-rotating carousels, animated graphics, marquees, or auto-refreshing content? Can users stop them?

criteria.ref: 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold (Level A)
criteria.text: Web pages do not contain anything that flashes more than three times in any one second period, or the flash is below the general flash and red flash thresholds.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does anything in your product flash, strobe, or rapidly change colours in a way that could trigger photosensitive seizures?

criteria.text: A mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple Web pages.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do you have a 'Skip to main content' link (or equivalent landmark structure) that lets keyboard users skip repeated navigation?

criteria.text: Web pages have titles that describe topic or purpose.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does every page in your product have a unique, descriptive <title>? Or do all pages share a generic title like the product name only?

criteria.text: If a Web page can be navigated sequentially and the navigation sequences affect meaning or operation, focusable components receive focus in an order that preserves meaning and operability.
criteria.interviewQuestion: When you tab through a page, does focus move in a logical order (top-to-bottom, left-to-right, following the visual flow)? Or does it jump around because of positive tabindex or DOM mismatch?

criteria.text: The purpose of each link can be determined from the link text alone or from the link text together with its programmatically determined link context.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do any of your links say only 'click here', 'read more', or 'learn more' without enough surrounding context to know what they go to?

criteria.text: The default human language of each Web page can be programmatically determined.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does the root <html> element have a lang attribute set to the correct language code?

criteria.text: When any component receives focus, it does not initiate a change of context.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does focusing on any control trigger an unexpected change — navigating to a new page, opening a modal, submitting a form?

criteria.text: Changing the setting of any user interface component does not automatically cause a change of context unless the user has been advised of the behavior before using the component.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does selecting a value in a dropdown or checking a checkbox auto-submit a form, navigate elsewhere, or otherwise change context without warning?

criteria.text: If an input error is automatically detected, the item that is in error is identified and the error is described to the user in text.
criteria.interviewQuestion: When form validation fails, are errors described in text — not only colour, not only icon? Can a screen-reader user know which field is wrong and why?

criteria.text: Labels or instructions are provided when content requires user input.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does every form field have a visible label (or placeholder + accessible name)? Are formatting rules (date format, password requirements) communicated before submission?

criteria.text: In content implemented using markup languages, elements have complete start and end tags, are nested according to their specifications, do not contain duplicate attributes, and any IDs are unique.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your HTML validate? In particular, no duplicate IDs (common in dynamic apps that mount the same component twice), no unclosed tags?

criteria.text: For all user interface components, the name and role can be programmatically determined; states, properties, and values can be programmatically set; and notification of changes is available to user agents, including assistive technologies.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are custom widgets (custom dropdowns, tabs, dialogs) built with proper ARIA roles and states? Does a screen reader announce them correctly as they're operated?

chapters.title: WCAG 2.0 Level AA Success Criteria
chapters.description: All Level AA criteria are required by Section 508 for software and web content.

criteria.text: Captions are provided for all live audio content in synchronized media.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your product stream live audio or video (webinars, live training, video calls)? If so, are captions provided in real time?

criteria.text: Audio description is provided for all prerecorded video content in synchronized media.
criteria.interviewQuestion: For prerecorded videos where visual information is not described in the dialogue, is there an audio description track?

criteria.text: The visual presentation of text and images of text has a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1, except for large text (3:1), incidental text, and logotypes.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your product meet WCAG AA contrast — 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text? Any known low-contrast areas (placeholder text, disabled-but-still-readable states, hover states)?

criteria.text: Text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent without loss of content or functionality.
criteria.interviewQuestion: At 200% browser zoom (or OS-level text scaling), does your UI still work — no clipped text, no overlapping controls, no horizontal scroll on what should be a vertical-only page?

criteria.text: If technologies can achieve the visual presentation, text is used to convey information rather than images of text, with limited exceptions.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Do you use images of text where real text would have worked — buttons rendered as images, screenshots with embedded text in marketing pages?

criteria.text: More than one way is available to locate a Web page within a set of Web pages.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are there multiple ways to find a given page — search, site map, breadcrumbs, navigation menu? At least two should be present (a step in a process is exempt).

criteria.text: Headings and labels describe topic or purpose.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are headings and labels meaningful — do they describe what follows? Avoid headings like 'Section 1' or 'Details' without further context.

criteria.text: Any keyboard operable user interface has a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator is visible.
criteria.interviewQuestion: When you tab through your UI, is there always a clearly visible focus indicator on the current element? Sometimes CSS `outline: none` removes this without a replacement.

criteria.text: The human language of each passage or phrase in the content can be programmatically determined, except for proper names, technical terms, words of indeterminate language, and words or phrases that have become part of the vernacular.
criteria.interviewQuestion: If your UI mixes languages — e.g. a French quote inside an English page — is the inline element marked with the correct lang attribute?

criteria.text: Navigational mechanisms that are repeated on multiple Web pages occur in the same relative order each time they are repeated, unless a change is initiated by the user.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Does your main navigation appear in the same place and same order on every page? Or does it reorder depending on context?

criteria.text: Components that have the same functionality within a set of Web pages are identified consistently.
criteria.interviewQuestion: Are functionally identical controls labelled consistently — is the same action always called 'Save' rather than 'Save' here and 'Submit' there?

criteria.text: If an input error is automatically detected and suggestions for correction are known, then the suggestions are provided to the user, unless it would jeopardize the security or purpose of the content.
criteria.interviewQuestion: When form validation fails, do error messages tell the user how to fix the problem — not just 'invalid' but 'must be a valid email address' or 'password must include a number'?

criteria.text: For Web pages that cause legal commitments or financial transactions for the user to occur, that modify or delete user-controllable data, or that submit user test responses, submissions are reversible, checked for input errors, or confirmed before submission.
criteria.interviewQuestion: For destructive or consequential actions — delete an item, finalize a report, run a billed scan — is there a confirmation step, reversibility, or input checking before the action completes?
